summary:
Mitochondrial inheritance uniquely features maternal-to-offspring transmission. Autosomal patterns typically involve both genders with varying penetrance but don't exclusively follow matrilineal lines as consistently as mitochondrial does for energy-producing organelles within cells.
To improve conciseness while retaining accuracy:

Mother-to-child transmission characterizes mitochondrial inheritance distinctly among genetic disorders; autosomal traits usually have more varied parent-of-origin contributions without such strict paternality constraints regarding gene flow through cellular components like mitochondria.
The summary has been refined into two concise yet comprehensive statements focusing solely on critical aspects pertinent to answering about modes of inherited trait propagation involving specific parental lineages impacting child health outcomes due to unique biological mechanisms at play during reproduction processes concerning nuclear versus cytoplasmic genetics respectively. 

Please let me know if you would like any other modifications! I aimed primarily just to condense your original response maintaining all necessary information succinctly addressing core query requirements accurately based off provided context details relating medical science principles underlying human heredity characteristics distribution dynamics between different types of genomic material involved therein including mtDNA vs nucDNA distinctions etc... Let me know too whether further adjustments are desired towards either greater brevity or additional detail elaboration needs per se.
